name: Approve requests with Microsoft Teams and PostgreSQL 24/7

parameters.errorMessage: Missing required field 'title' on incoming task.

parameters.query:
INSERT INTO task_log (taskId, title, assignee, logged_at)
VALUES ($1, $2, $3, NOW());

parameters.text: =✅ Approve requests with Microsoft Teams and PostgreSQL 24/7 — run completed at {{ $now.toFormat('HH:mm') }}

parameters.content:
### New Task Form Submission

Serves an n8n-hosted form (Title, Assignee, Duedate). Each submission becomes one execution.

Next: Required Fields Present?

### Required Fields Present?

Two-way gate on `={{ $json.title }}`. TRUE continues the happy path, FALSE takes the alternate branch.

Ports: ①→ Route by Work Type · ②→ Abort — Malformed Input

### Abort — Malformed Input

Fails fast with a clear message; triggers the error workflow instead of processing bad data.

### Route by Work Type

Routes each item to one of 3 lanes: auto-approve, needs-review + fallback.

Ports: ①→ Process 'auto-approve' → PostgreSQL · ②→ Fast-Track 'needs-review' + Alert · ③→ Hold Unknown Types (DLQ)

### Process 'auto-approve' → PostgreSQL

Runs a parameterized `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT` upsert — parameters are bound via `queryReplacement`, never string-interpolated, so it's immune to SQL injection.

Ports: ①→ Merge Routed Work · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Postgres connection

### Fast-Track 'needs-review' + Alert

Posts a message to Slack (`#projects`) — a low-latency way for the team to see automation activity without checking a dashboard.

Ports: ①→ Merge Routed Work · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Slack OAuth2

### Hold Unknown Types (DLQ)

Runs a parameterized SQL statement against Postgres — values are bound via `queryReplacement`, not string-interpolated, so it's immune to SQL injection.

Ports: ①→ Merge Routed Work · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Postgres connection

### Merge Routed Work

Rejoins 3 branches into one stream (mode: append) so downstream logic runs once.

Next: Align Field Names

### Align Field Names

Normalizes field names so every source feeds the same downstream schema.

Next: Save Execution Metadata

### Save Execution Metadata

Saves key fields onto the execution record — searchable later in n8n's execution list.

Next: Sort Tasks Newest First

### Sort Tasks Newest First

Orders items (newest first) so limits and digests pick the most relevant records.

Next: Send Summary to Slack

### Send Summary to Slack

Posts a message to Slack (`#projects`) — a low-latency way for the team to see automation activity without checking a dashboard.

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Slack OAuth2

### Capture Failure Context

Shapes the item: writes `failedNode`, `errorMessage`, `workflow`. Keeps the schema explicit between stages.

Next: Escalate Failure to Microsoft Teams

### Escalate Failure to Microsoft Teams

Posts a chat message into a Microsoft Teams channel — same role as a Slack notify, for teams standardized on Teams.

↻ retries 3×/2s · 🔑 Microsoft OAuth2

nodes.name: Note: Escalate Failure to Microsoft Teams

parameters.content:
# Approve requests with Microsoft Teams and PostgreSQL 24/7

**Pattern:** Approval Workflow · **Level:** Core · **Trigger:** Form

## What it does
Approve requests with Microsoft Teams and PostgreSQL 24/7. It starts from a hosted form submission.

## Why it exists
Manual standups is slow and error-prone; this workflow makes the process repeatable, monitored and hands-off, with failures surfaced instead of silently lost.

## Main flow
New Task Form Submission → Required Fields Present? → Route by Work Type → Process 'auto-approve' → PostgreSQL → Merge Routed Work → Align Field Names → Save Execution Metadata → …

## Use it for
- Teams that want to approval workflow without manual effort
- Anyone automating standups in software development
- Replacing a fragile manual process with a reliable workflow

## You get
- Responds in seconds
- Saves hours of manual work each week
- Has retries and error handling built in

## Before first run
Connect credentials: Microsoft OAuth2; Postgres connection; Slack OAuth2.
Follow the color key: 🟦 entry · 🟪 AI · 🟫 actions · ⬜ utilities · 🟩 outputs · 🟥 error rail.
Each node has its own doc card underneath — read those before editing.

## Try it now
Sample data is pinned on the trigger — hit **Execute Workflow** to watch it flow through every node before connecting any real credentials. Unpin the trigger (right-click → Unpin) when you're ready to go live.

## Design decisions

Unrecognized work types are parked in a dead-letter lane instead of erroring the whole run or silently dropping the item — new event kinds will show up eventually, and this is where you'd notice one without losing it. One kind is deliberately routed through a sub-workflow to show how heavier per-type logic can be extracted without touching this router at all.

## Practice exercises

Once it's wired up with real credentials, try:

1. Send a work-type value that matches no switch rule and confirm it lands in the dead-letter branch.
2. Add a new work type and its own handling branch.
3. Replace the direct-write branch with a sub-workflow call, matching the pattern already used for the first kind.

## 🛟 Error handling & retries

External calls retry **3× with 2s backoff**. Anything still failing exits through its node's error output into the red error rail → context is captured and the on-call channel is alerted. Set an n8n **Error Workflow** in settings as the final safety net.

## 🛠 Customize me

Quick wins: point the data nodes at your own accounts (see 🔑 lines on each card), adjust the trigger cadence, and tune thresholds/labels to your standups process. Every node card explains its role — safe to extend at any marked branch.
